title: Frankenstein; Or, The Modern Prometheus
author: Shelley, Mary Wollstonecraft
summary: Mary Shelley recounts the genesis of Frankenstein during the wet summer of 1816, when she, Percy Shelley, Lord Byron, and others challenged one another to write ghost stories. Influenced by German tales, conversations about the principle of life, Dr. Darwin’s vermicelli, and galvanism, she dreamed of a pale student reanimating a hideous corpse. Haunted by that vision, she resolved to write a narrative to thrill readers, beginning "It was on a dreary night of November." Shelley emphasizes invention’s dependence on existing ideas, credits her husband’s encouragement, and explains stylistic revisions for the 1831 edition. She frames it as human passion exploration.

characters.name: Mary W. Shelley
characters.age: young girl when idea occurred; adult when publishing
characters.lifespan: author of the work; composes introduction and preface; publishes revised 1831 edition

characters.name: Percy (her husband)
characters.lifespan: present as inciter and adviser; referenced as companion the author will never see again

characters.name: Lord Byron
characters.lifespan: resident near the Shelleys in Switzerland during 1816; participant in the storytelling challenge

characters.name: John William Polidori
characters.lifespan: present during the gathering; attempted a tale (referred to as "Poor Polidori")

characters.name: Dr. Darwin (mentioned)
characters.lifespan: referenced for alleged experiment with vermicelli and discussions about life

characters.name: The pale student of unhallowed arts
characters.lifespan: creates and then is horrified by a reanimated corpse; awakens to find the creature at his bedside

characters.name: The hideous phantom / creature
characters.personality: hideous, grotesque, with yellow watery speculative eyes
characters.lifespan: brought to transient life by the student's experiment; exists as the central horrid vision

characters.name: The Inconstant Lover (story)
characters.lifespan: appears in the German tales that influenced the group

characters.name: The sinful founder (story)
characters.lifespan: appears in the translated ghost stories; bestows deathly kiss upon younger sons

characters.name: The skull-headed lady (Polidori's idea)
characters.lifespan: Polidori's fragmentary tale ends with her despatched to the tomb of the Capulets

characters.name: My childhood's companion and friend
characters.lifespan: referred to by the author as an important early reader; unnamed in the excerpt

characters.name: The publishers
characters.lifespan: commission the introduction and publish the Standard Novels edition

locations.name: Northern shores of the Tay (near Dundee), Scotland
The Alps (white high Alps; visible from the lake)

unusual_words.definition: to speak or write at length; expand upon
unusual_words.context: Mary describes being asked how she came to "dilate upon" so hideous an idea.

unusual_words.definition: having a strong dislike or opposition
unusual_words.context: She writes that she is "very averse to bringing myself forward in print."

unusual_words.definition: an unwelcome act of entering or imposing
unusual_words.context: She refers to appearing in print as a "personal intrusion."

unusual_words.definition: wrote hastily or carelessly
unusual_words.context: She recalls that "as a child I scribbled" and wrote stories.

unusual_words.definition: an activity done for enjoyment
unusual_words.context: Her "favourite pastime" was to write stories during recreation.

unusual_words.definition: leisure activity; play
unusual_words.context: She mentions "hours given me for recreation."

unusual_words.definition: occurring while awake (in contrast to sleeping dreams)
unusual_words.context: She distinguishes her "waking dreams" from her writings.

unusual_words.definition: series of thoughts or images in sleep or imaginative reverie
unusual_words.context: She describes her "dreams" as more fantastic than her prose.

unusual_words.definition: a succession of thoughts
unusual_words.context: She speaks of "trains of thought" following one another.

unusual_words.definition: existing only in the imagination
unusual_words.context: Her "imaginary incidents" populated her waking hours.

unusual_words.definition: fanciful; extremely odd or imaginative
unusual_words.context: Her dreams were "at once more fantastic and agreeable than my writings."

unusual_words.definition: pleasant; enjoyable
unusual_words.context: She calls some of her mental flights "agreeable."

unusual_words.definition: one who copies another's style
unusual_words.context: She confesses she was a "close imitator" in early writing.

unusual_words.definition: ideas or proposals put forward
unusual_words.context: She mentions "suggestions of my own mind."

unusual_words.definition: a place or state of shelter or protection
unusual_words.context: Her dreams were "my refuge when annoyed."

unusual_words.definition: the act of looking back on past events
unusual_words.context: "Blank and dreary on retrospection I call them;" she writes of childhood places.

unusual_words.definition: eagle's nest; elevated retreat (variant of eyrie/aery)
unusual_words.context: She calls the shores "the eyry of freedom."

unusual_words.definition: not noticed or paid attention to
unusual_words.context: "unheeded I could commune with the creatures of my fancy."

unusual_words.definition: to converse or interact intimately with
unusual_words.context: She could "commune with the creatures of my fancy."

unusual_words.definition: imagination or whimsical notion
unusual_words.context: She repeatedly refers to her "fancy" and its creations.

unusual_words.definition: done regularly or repeatedly
unusual_words.context: She notes her "habitual residence" on the northern shores of the Tay.

unusual_words.definition: bare, desolate, cold
unusual_words.context: She mentions "the bleak sides of the woodless mountains."

unusual_words.definition: without trees; treeless
unusual_words.context: She describes "woodless mountains" near her childhood home.

unusual_words.definition: creations or works of writing
unusual_words.context: She calls her inventive mental flights her "true compositions."

unusual_words.definition: light, delicate, or insubstantial
unusual_words.context: She speaks of the "airy flights of my imagination."

unusual_words.definition: female protagonist
unusual_words.context: She says "I did not make myself the heroine of my tales."

unusual_words.definition: idealized, adventurous, or emotionally charged
unusual_words.context: She references "romantic woes" as unlikely to be her lot.

unusual_words.definition: the fact of being who one is
unusual_words.context: She remarks upon "my own identity" when discussing herself in stories.

unusual_words.definition: to register or enter in a list (British spelling enrol)
unusual_words.context: Her husband urged her to "enrol myself on the page of fame."

unusual_words.definition: encouraging or urging on
unusual_words.context: He was "for ever inciting me to obtain literary reputation."

unusual_words.definition: having no particular interest or sympathy
unusual_words.context: She says she has since become "infinitely indifferent" to fame.

unusual_words.definition: journeying from place to place
unusual_words.context: "Travelling, and the cares of a family, occupied my time."

unusual_words.definition: refined, educated, or developed
unusual_words.context: She contrasts her own mind with his "far more cultivated mind."

unusual_words.definition: people living near another
unusual_words.context: They "became the neighbours of Lord Byron" in Switzerland.

unusual_words.definition: a principal division of a long poem
unusual_words.context: Byron was writing the "third canto of Childe Harold."

unusual_words.definition: dressed or covered
unusual_words.context: Byron's lines are "clothed in all the light and harmony of poetry."

unusual_words.definition: not genial; inhospitable or bleak
unusual_words.context: She describes 1816 as a "wet, ungenial summer."

unusual_words.definition: continuing without pause or interruption
unusual_words.context: "incessant rain often confined us for days to the house."

unusual_words.definition: converted from one language into another
unusual_words.context: She and company read "volumes of ghost stories, translated from the German."

unusual_words.definition: a thin pasta (used in anecdote about Dr. Darwin)
unusual_words.context: Dr. Darwin supposedly preserved a piece of "vermicelli" that moved.

unusual_words.definition: the application of electricity to stimulate muscles; early electrical experimentation
unusual_words.context: "galvanism had given token of such things" as reanimation.

unusual_words.definition: brought back to life
unusual_words.context: She contemplates that "a corpse would be re-animated."

unusual_words.definition: one part of a larger whole
unusual_words.context: She imagines "the component parts of a creature" manufactured together.

unusual_words.definition: made or assembled, especially industrially or artificially
unusual_words.context: "component parts of a creature might be manufactured, brought together."

unusual_words.definition: relating to life; essential to life
unusual_words.context: Such parts might be "endued with vital warmth."

unusual_words.definition: heat or life-giving heat; figuratively vitality
unusual_words.context: "endued with vital warmth" in speculative experiments.

unusual_words.definition: mysterious or magical; here meaning the late hour
unusual_words.context: She mentions that "Night waned upon this talk, and even the witching hour had gone by."

unusual_words.definition: not invited or requested; spontaneous
unusual_words.context: "My imagination, unbidden, possessed and guided me."

unusual_words.definition: bending the knee in submission or supplication
unusual_words.context: She saw "the pale student of unhallowed arts kneeling beside the thing he had put together."

unusual_words.definition: not consecrated; sinful or profane
unusual_words.context: The vision included a "pale student of unhallowed arts."

unusual_words.definition: a ghost or apparition; an illusion
unusual_words.context: She saw "the hideous phantasm of a man stretched out."

unusual_words.definition: extremely impressive or large
unusual_words.context: She warns against attempts to "mock the stupendous mechanism of the Creator."

unusual_words.definition: a system of parts working together; the structure of something
unusual_words.context: "stupendous mechanism of the Creator of the world."

unusual_words.definition: extremely unpleasant; repulsive
unusual_words.context: The artist would rush from his "odious handywork."

unusual_words.definition: work done by hand; creation
unusual_words.context: "odious handywork" describes the cobbled-together creation.

unusual_words.definition: a place or origin of something; here used metaphorically for the seat of life
unusual_words.context: "the cradle of life" referring to the animated corpse.

unusual_words.definition: an intense feeling of fear, shock, or disgust
unusual_words.context: She sought to "awaken thrilling horror" in the reader.

unusual_words.definition: to cause to thicken or develop unpleasantly (figurative: make blood curdle)
unusual_words.context: Her aim was to "curdle the blood" of readers.

unusual_words.definition: to cause to speed up; here to make heartbeat faster
unusual_words.context: "quicken the beatings of the heart."

unusual_words.definition: causing embarrassment or shame
unusual_words.context: "I was forced to reply with a mortifying negative."

unusual_words.definition: lack of ability or power
unusual_words.context: She speaks of "blank incapability of invention."

unusual_words.definition: calls for help or inspiration
unusual_words.context: "when dull Nothing replies to our anxious invocations."

unusual_words.definition: relating to Sancho (allusion); used as an adjectival reference to Sancho Panza-style phrase
unusual_words.context: "to speak in Sanchean phrase;" she uses the allusion in discussing beginnings.

unusual_words.definition: an archaic spelling of Hindus (people of India)
unusual_words.context: "The Hindoos give the world an elephant to support it" in a metaphor she cites.

unusual_words.definition: large mammal; used here in a cosmological metaphor
unusual_words.context: She recounts the Hindoos' cosmological image of an "elephant to support" the world.

unusual_words.definition: a slow-moving land reptile; in the metaphor supporting the elephant
unusual_words.context: The elephant is said to "stand upon a tortoise."

unusual_words.definition: Christopher Columbus, used in the idiom about ingenuity
unusual_words.context: She invokes the story of "Columbus and his egg."

unusual_words.definition: the reproductive body of birds; used in the Columbus anecdote
unusual_words.context: "Columbus and his egg" illustrates invention's nature.

unusual_words.definition: one who writes novels
unusual_words.context: "the most humble novelist" may claim a licence in fiction.

unusual_words.definition: freedom to deviate from strict rules (British spelling)
unusual_words.context: She speaks of applying a "licence" to prose fiction.

unusual_words.definition: to belong to or be connected with
unusual_words.context: "in the matters of discovery and invention that appertain to the imagination."

unusual_words.definition: describing or portraying in detail
unusual_words.context: She refers to "delineating of human passions."

unusual_words.definition: including much or covering broadly
unusual_words.context: "more comprehensive and commanding than any which ordinary relations can yield."

unusual_words.definition: Homer's epic poem (classical reference)
unusual_words.context: She cites classical models like "The Iliad."

unusual_words.definition: Shakespeare's play The Tempest (literary reference)
unusual_words.context: She mentions "Shakspeare, in the Tempest," as an example.

unusual_words.definition: referring to "A Midsummer Night's Dream" by Shakespeare
unusual_words.context: She references "Midsummer Night's Dream" among poetic models.

unusual_words.definition: John Milton, author of Paradise Lost (literary reference)
unusual_words.context: She cites "Milton, in Paradise Lost," as another model.

unusual_words.definition: in context, part of the title "Paradise Lost"
unusual_words.context: "Milton, in Paradise Lost," provides an example of imaginative licence.

unusual_words.definition: relating to tragedy or serious suffering
unusual_words.context: She references "the tragic poetry of Greece."

unusual_words.definition: introductory remarks at the beginning of a book
unusual_words.context: She notes that her husband largely wrote the "preface."

unusual_words.definition: light in color; wan; used to describe ghostly figures
unusual_words.context: "the pale student" and "the pale ghost" appear in her retellings.

unusual_words.definition: the visor of a helmet or piece of armour (archaic usage)
unusual_words.context: A ghost is "clothed...in complete armour, but with the beaver up."

unusual_words.definition: the Capulet family from Romeo and Juliet (proper noun used in anecdote)
unusual_words.context: Polidori's fragment sends his skull-headed lady "to the tomb of the Capulets."

unusual_words.definition: occurring in irregular bursts; intermittent
unusual_words.context: "the moon's fitful beams" illuminate the apparition.

unusual_words.definition: a walkway or approach lined with trees
unusual_words.context: A "gloomy avenue" is part of a translated tale she recalls.

unusual_words.definition: leaned or inclined (past tense of bend)
unusual_words.context: "Eternal sorrow sat upon his face as he bent down and kissed the forehead."

unusual_words.definition: shriveled or faded (as from loss of vitality)
unusual_words.context: "withered like flowers snapt upon the stalk."

unusual_words.definition: archaic past tense of snap; broken off suddenly
unusual_words.context: "snapt upon the stalk" describes the withering of the youths.

unusual_words.definition: an incomplete piece or portion of a larger work
unusual_words.context: Byron "began a tale, a fragment of which he printed" with Mazeppa.

unusual_words.definition: a remark that is dull or lacking originality
unusual_words.context: She notes the poets were "annoyed by the platitude of prose."

unusual_words.definition: not pleasant or sympathetic; unsuitable
unusual_words.context: "the uncongenial task" of composing prose ghost stories for the poets.

unusual_words.definition: occupied oneself with an activity
unusual_words.context: "I busied myself to think of a story."

unusual_words.definition: boring or annoying through repetition
unusual_words.context: She calls the task the "tiresome unlucky ghost story."

unusual_words.definition: without success; fruitlessly
unusual_words.context: "I thought and pondered--vainly."

unusual_words.definition: empty or void; here describing incapability
unusual_words.context: "blank incapability of invention" when nothing comes.

unusual_words.definition: causing horror or disgust
unusual_words.context: She repeatedly uses "horrid" to describe the imagined creation.

unusual_words.definition: the area beside a bed
unusual_words.context: In her dream the "horrid thing stands at his bedside."

unusual_words.definition: hangings at a window or bed
unusual_words.context: "opening his curtains, and looking on him with yellow, watery...eyes."

unusual_words.definition: containing, resembling, or filled with water; here pale and wet-looking
unusual_words.context: "yellow, watery, but speculative eyes" of the creature.

unusual_words.definition: based on conjecture or theory rather than knowledge
unusual_words.context: The creature is described as having "speculative eyes."
